S33: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Imbalance of gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) and related modulators has been implicated as an important factor in the pathogenesis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), which is also known as motor neuron disease (MND).
S34: In this context, the role and mechanism of action of gabapentin and baclofen have been extensively investigated, although with conflicting results.
S35: This is the first systematic review to assess clinical trials of GABA modulators for the treatment of ALS.
S36: Objectives To examine the eﬀicacy of gabapentin, baclofen, or other GABA modulators in delaying the progression of ALS, and to evaluate adverse eﬀects of these interventions.
S37: Search methods On 16 August 2016, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL Plus, AMED, and LILACS.
S38: In addition, we checked the bibliographies of the trials found in order to identify any other trials, and contacted trial authors to identify relevant unpublished results or additional clinical trials.
S39: On 30 August 2016, we searched two clinical trials registries.
S40: Selection criteria Types of studies: double-blind randomized controlled trials (RCTs) or quasi-RCTs Types of participants: adults with a diagnosis of probable or definite ALS Types of interventions: gabapentin, baclofen, or other GABA modulators compared with placebo, no treatment, or each other Primary outcome: survival at one year from study enrollment Secondary outcomes: individual rate of decline of maximum voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC), expressed as arm megascore; rate of decline of per cent predicted forced vital capacity (FVC); rate of decline of ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS); health-related quality of life; survival evaluated by pooling hazards; and adverse events Gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) modulators for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2017 The Cochrane Collaboration.

S44: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Data collection and analysis At least two review authors independently checked titles and abstracts identified by the searches.
S45: The review authors obtained and independently analyzed original individual participant data from each included study; additional review authors and the Cochrane Neuromuscular Managing Editor checked the outcome data.
S46: Two authors independently assessed the risk of bias in included studies.
S47: Main results We identified two double-blind RCTs of gabapentin treatment in ALS for inclusion in this review.
S48: We found no eligible RCTs of baclofen or other GABA modulators.
S49: The selected studies were phase II and phase III trials, which lasted six and nine months, respectively.
S50: They were highly comparable because both were comparisons of oral gabapentin and placebo, performed by the same investigators.
S51: The trials enrolled 355 participants with ALS: 80 in the gabapentin group and 72 in the placebo group in the first (phase II) trial and 101 in the gabapentin group and 102 in the placebo group in the second (phase III) trial.
S52: Neither trial was long enough to report survival at one year, which was our primary outcome.
S53: We found little or no diﬀerence in estimated one-year survival between the treated group and the placebo group (78% versus 77%, P = 0.63 by log-rank test; high-quality evidence).
S54: We also found little or no diﬀerence in the rate of decline of MVIC expressed as arm megascore, or rate of FVC decline (high-quality evidence).
S55: One trial investigated monthly decline in the ALSFRS and quality of life measured using the 12-Item Short Form Survey (SF-12) and found little or no diﬀerence between groups (moderate- quality evidence).
S56: The trials reported similar adverse events.
S57: Complaints that were clearly elevated in those taking gabapentin, based on analyses of the combined data, were light-headedness, drowsiness, and limb swelling (high-quality evidence).
S58: Fatigue and falls occurred more frequently with gabapentin than with placebo in one trial, but when we combined the data for fatigue from both trials, there was no clear diﬀerence between the groups.
S59: We assessed the overall risk of bias in the included trials as low.
S60: Authors' conclusions According to high-quality evidence, gabapentin is not eﬀective in treating ALS.
S61: It does not extend survival, slow the rate of decline of muscle strength, respiratory function and, based on moderate-quality evidence, probably does not improve quality of life or slow monthly decline in the ALSFRS.
S62: Other GABA modulators have not been studied in randomized trials.
S63: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Gamma aminobutyric acid modulators for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease Review question Do medicines that promote the eﬀects of the brain chemical gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) delay the progression of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)?
S64: Background ALS, which is also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a condition that aﬀects motor neurons in the brain and spinal cord.
S65: A person with ALS gradually loses the ability to control movement.
S66: In about two-thirds of people with ALS, the condition aﬀects the arms first, and diﬀiculty walking will follow.
S67: The muscles of the throat can also become weak, which aﬀects swallowing and speaking.
S68: As the disease progresses, muscle wasting worsens, with stiﬀness, cramping and loss of the ability to move.
S69: Death generally occurs within two to five years.
S70: Glutamate is a chemical mediator in the brain that stimulates motor neurons.
S71: Glutamate overproduction is probably responsible for motor neuron damage in ALS.
S72: The molecule GABA acts to lessen the eﬀects of glutamate.
S73: Medicines that increase GABA activity (GABA modulators), such as gabapentin and baclofen, are possible treatments for ALS.
S74: This is the first systematic review of the evidence from clinical trials of GABA modulators for ALS.
S75: Study characteristics A systematic search of the medical literature found two randomized trials of gabapentin compared with placebo (inactive treatment).
S76: The same team of scientists performed both trials, which were sponsored by the manufacturer.
S77: The trials involved a total of 355 people with ALS.
S78: Treatment with gabapentin lasted six and nine months.
S79: We found no trials of baclofen or other GABA modulators in ALS that met our selection criteria.
S80: We assessed the gabapentin trials as well run and well conducted.
S81: Key results and quality of the evidence Neither of the trials were long enough to for us to report survival at one year.
S82: Combined results from the two studies (based on 274 participants) provided high-quality evidence of little or no diﬀerence in estimated one-year survival, the rate of decline in respiratory function, or rate of decline in arm strength in people treated with gabapentin compared to those treated with placebo.
S83: One trial (128 participants) measured quality of life and monthly decline in function (measured by the ALS Functional Rating Scale).
S84: There was little or no diﬀerence in the ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS) or quality of life between the gabapentin group and the placebo group.

S89: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews People who received gabapentin had more light-headedness, drowsiness, and limb swelling than those taking placebo when we combined data from both trials (353 participants).
S90: Fatigue and falls occurred more frequently with gabapentin than with placebo in one trial, but when we combined the data for fatigue from both trials, there was no clear diﬀerence between the groups.
S91: In conclusion, high-quality evidence indicates that gabapentin does not extend survival or slow the rate of decline of muscle strength or respiratory function.
S92: Moderate-quality evidence shows no eﬀect on quality of life or decline in ALSFRS.
S93: Other GABA modulators have not been studied in randomized trials.

S136: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Description of the condition Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), which is also known as motor neuron disease (MND), Lou Gehrig's or Charcot's disease, is a neurodegenerative disorder characterized by loss of motor neurons of the cerebral cortex, brain stem, and spinal cord.
S137: The condition results in progressive weakness and paralysis of voluntary muscles, which ultimately leads to death.
S138: Median prevalence (per 100,000 population) has been estimated as 5.4 (interquartile range (IQR) 4.06 to 7.89) in Europe and 3.4 (IQR 3.15 to 3.65) in the USA (Chiò 2013).
S139: Although the cause of ALS remains unknown, so-called excitotoxicity induced by an imbalance of the excitatory neurotransmitter glutamate and inhibitory neurotransmitter gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA) appears to be important in pathogenesis, because excitotoxicity is thought to trigger irreversible cell damage (for a review, see Rao 2004).
S140: Glutamate has been suggested as a possible chemical insult for triggering the slow neuronal demise and related abnormalities in glutamate metabolism seen in ALS, which include elevated glutamate levels in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) (Plaitakis 1987; Rothstein 1991; Shaw 1995; Spreux-Varoquaux 2002).
S141: The discovery that GABA content is increased in the serum and CSF of people with ALS led investigators to consider its possible contribution to ALS pathogenesis (Niebroj-Dobosz 1999).
S142: In addition, anatomical analysis of human postmortem brains revealed a significantly reduced expression of messenger ribonucleic acid (mRNA) for the GABA receptor subunit (alpha1- subunit) (Petri 2003).
S143: These findings correspond with those of a positron emission tomography study using the ligand flumazenil that showed downregulation of GABA receptors, which is likely to reflect a loss of interneuronal function (Lloyd 2000).
S144: However, electrophysiological measurements revealed no correlation between the ALS hyperexcitability index in diﬀerent motor units and changes in serum and CSF levels of GABA (Kostera 2002).
S145: Description of the intervention Pharmacological reversal of cortical hyperexcitability in people with ALS has been observed afer sustained treatment with GABA modulators such as gabapentin (Caramia 2000), even though experiments with rat neocortical slices found no eﬀect of gabapentin on GABA release because of the selective activation of presynaptic GABA-B heteroreceptors (Parker 2004).
S146: Baclofen is a GABA agonist with some eﬀicacy in reducing spasticity of cerebral or spinal origin in people with ALS (Norris 1979).
S147: How the intervention might work The above studies indicate that the mechanism of action of gabapentin is still controversial (Taylor 1994; Taylor 1998).
S148: They provided a rationale for undertaking clinical trials of gabapentin in ALS, despite the fact that gabapentin is not a structural analog of GABA (Satzinger 1994), but simply a compound with conformational properties that mimic its amino acid structure (Bryans 1999).
S149: Although the evidence supporting a role for GABA is under debate, suﬀicient data exist to justify trials of the GABA agonist baclofen or the GABA modulator gabapentin in ALS.
S150: Why it is important to do this review While treatment for spasticity and cramps in ALS have been the subjects of other reviews (Ashworth 2012; Baldinger 2012), no systematic review has addressed the benefit of GABA modulators such as gabapentin or baclofen in slowing the clinical progression of ALS.
S151: Thus, the specific aim of this review is to assess the clinical impact of GABA modulators as disease-modifying agents.

S153: M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies We considered for inclusion double-blind, randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-RCTs of gabapentin, baclofen, or other GABA modulators in the treatment of ALS.
S154: Quasi-RCTs use methods of allocation that are partly systematic, such as allocation by alternate days, date of birth, or hospital number.
S155: Types of participants We considered for inclusion adults with a clinical and laboratory- supported diagnosis of probable or definite ALS that conformed to the criteria of the World Federation of Neurology (WFN) Research Group (Brooks 2000).
S156: Types of interventions We considered for inclusion gabapentin, baclofen, or other GABA modulators compared with placebo, no treatment, or each other.

S165: Health-related quality of life assessed by means of a validated multipurpose scale, such as the 36-Item Short Form Health Survey (Rand 2016).

S180: Search methods for identification of studies Electronic searches On 16 August 2016, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) in the in the Cochrane Register of Studies (CRS) online, MEDLINE (January 1966 to August 2016), Embase (January 1980 to August 2016), CINAHL Plus (Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature; January 1982 to August 2016), AMED (Allied and Complementary Medicine; January 1985 to August 2016), and LILACS (Latin American and Caribbean Health Science Information database; January 1982 to August 2016).
S181: On 30 August 2016, we also searched US National Institutes of Health Ongoing Trials Register ClinicalTrials.gov and the World Health Organization International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (ICTRP; www.who.int/ictrp/en/).
S182: The detailed search strategies are in the appendices: Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register (Appendix 1), MEDLINE (Appendix 2), Embase (Appendix 3), AMED (Appendix 4), CINAHL Plus (Appendix 5), LILACS (Appendix 6), CENTRAL (Appendix 7), ClinicalTrials.gov (Appendix 8), and ICTRP (Appendix 9).
S183: Searching other resources We checked the bibliographies of the trials found in order to identify references to other trials.
S184: In addition, we contacted authors of trials to identify relevant unpublished results or clinical trials.
S185: Data collection and analysis Selection of studies Two or three review authors (from among AD, DHM and RP) independently checked all titles and abstracts identified by the searches.
S186: The same review authors independently examined the full-text reports of the collected papers in order to select trials that fit the inclusion criteria for the review.
S187: There was no disagreement on study selection.
S188: We included a PRISMA flow chart to illustrate the study selection process and noted the reasons for study exclusion in suﬀicient detail for completion of a Characteristics of excluded studies table.
S189: Data extraction and management We were able to obtain the original data for each participant in all included trials from the trial authors, two of whom are authors of this review.
S190: For each study, we had original raw data for every participant for all outcome endpoints.
S191: We then combined all original data into one dataset, which facilitated statistical analyses.
S192: A statistician independent of the trials (AO'K) and the Managing Editor of Cochrane Neuromuscular double-checked the data in the review to ensure accuracy.
S193: Assessment of risk of bias in included studies Two review authors (AD and RP) independently assessed the risk of bias in each included study.
S194: We would have resolved disagreement by consensus, but none occurred.
S195: Each review author assessed the risk of bias of the studies, taking into account the following parameters: security of randomization, allocation concealment, blinding of participants and personnel, blinding of outcome assessors, completeness of outcome data, selective outcome reporting, and other sources of bias, according to the methods set out in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).
S196: We made judgements for each domain of low, high, or unclear risk of bias.
S197: We included a 'Risk of bias' summary figure showing the assessments for the included studies (Figure 1).

S207: Statistical methods We obtained data from the principal investigators for each participant in each of the clinical trials included in this review.
S208: For survival, we combined the data from the studies into a single data file and used the Kaplan-Meier method to estimate survival at 12 months afer enrollment into the trial.
S209: We also used the log-rank statistic to test whether participants treated with gabapentin had longer survival than those treated with placebo.
S210: Both studies used rate of decline in arm megascore strength (MVIC) of eight arm muscle groups (bilateral shoulder and elbow flexion and extension) as their primary measure of eﬀicacy.
S211: Both studies measured MVIC every four weeks.
S212: Raw scores for MVIC were standardized to published norms using z-score transformations (raw score minus norm average divided by norm standard deviation).
S213: The arm megascore was calculated as the average z- score over all tested muscles for each participant at each visit.
S214: The primary outcome measure was the mean slope of the arm megascores (rate of decrease over time, measured in days from initial visit), for each participant in the intent-to-treat population, which included all participants randomized to a study medication (active or placebo) and having at least two visits to the center.
S215: In the original analyses of each study a slope was estimated by linear regression for each participant and mean slopes for treated and placebo were compared using t-test or Mann-Whitney test.
S216: These analyses weighted each participant equally, regardless of the number of times each participant was evaluated.
S217: In this meta-analysis, we pooled individual participant data from the trials and fit a linear mixed eﬀects (lme) model to the combined data.
S218: This model takes into account the number of times each participant is evaluated so that those with more evaluations receive greater weight when estimating model parameters.
S219: The lme model also estimates pooled variances rather than separate ones when each participant is fit separately using linear regression.

S225: We also use the lme model to fit combined FVC data.
S226: Our statistical model assumed that arm megascore (transformed to average z-score) and FVC declined linearly over time.
S227: When fitting the pooled data, the lme model included fixed-eﬀect terms for intercept (starting value), slope (rate of decline over time), and change in slope for gabapentin treatment.
S228: Random eﬀects for intercept and slope allowed for person-to-person diﬀerences in slope and intercept and an additional random eﬀect for each participant's deviation from the linear fit.
S229: The model is given mathematically by the formula: Yij = (B0 + b0i) + (B1 + B2*Ii + b1i)*Tij + eij, where i indicates participant and Tij indicates time (month of the jth measurement in the ith participant).
S230: Capital letters indicate fixed- eﬀect coeﬀicients and lower case letters are random eﬀects.
S231: The term Ii is an indicator equal to 1 for the ith participant on active treatment and 0 for placebo.
S232: We also added fixed-eﬀect terms to test whether treatment eﬀects diﬀered by trial, symptom duration, and initial arm megascore.
S233: We used Stata version 11.0 for the above statistical analyses (Stata 2009).
S234: Other analyses We calculated a risk ratio (RR) with 95% CI for adverse event outcomes using the Cochrane statistical sofware Review Manager 5 (RevMan) (RevMan 2014).
S235: We used a fixed-eﬀect model for these analyses as the two included trials were performed with similar methods by the same investigators, and we consider them very likely to be measuring the same eﬀects.
S236: We assessed heterogeneity in the meta-analyses visually and using the Chi2 test and I2 statistic calculated by RevMan.
S237: We followed guidance for rule of thumb interpretation of I2 in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).

S266: Both were randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind studies based on the enrollment of people with a diagnosis of definite or probable ALS.

S268: During Miller 2001, participants received oral gabapentin 1200 mg three times daily or placebo for nine months.

S270: We excluded a nonblinded study on the grounds that randomized participants received diﬀerent doses and schedules of gabapentin, and a nonrandomized group formed the control group (Mazzini 1998).

S272: The data were too meagre for analysis.

S275: The Miller 1996 report did not describe allocation concealment; therefore, we rated this domain and blinding of participants as unclear.

S281: This report also noted a diﬀerence in arm megascore rate of decline in the placebo groups between the two trials.
S282: To account for these factors, we added terms to the lme model to account for diﬀerences in slopes between the two trials and for symptom duration.

S318: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews *log-rank test stratified by trial in combined data Adverse events The two trials reported adverse events, which were similar in kind.
S319: When we combined data from both trials (N = 353; high- quality evidence), complaints that were elevated in those taking gabapentin were light-headedness (RR 2.80, 95% CI 1.79 to 4.40), drowsiness (RR 2.64, 95% CI 1.61 to 4.33), and limb swelling (RR 2.70, 95% CI 1.45 to 5.02).
S320: Falls occurred more frequently with gabapentin than placebo in Miller 2001, but not in Miller 1996 (RR from the combined analysis 1.65, 95% CI 1.06 to 2.57).
S321: Miller 2001 reported only these adverse events, which were those that occurred with a significantly greater frequency in the gabapentin group.
S322: We have also presented the other adverse events reported in Miller 1996 with a greater than five per cent frequency in gabapentin or placebo groups (Analysis 1.1).
S323: Miller 1996 (N = 149) reported a greater frequency of fatigue with gabapentin than with placebo (RR 3.84, 95% CI 1.14 to 12.92) and a lower frequency of headache with gabapentin than with placebo (RR 0.51, 95% CI 0.26 to 1.00), with little or no diﬀerence between the two groups in other recorded events.
S324: No important heterogeneity was apparent in the adverse event meta-analyses.
S325: D I S C U S S I O N Summary of main results We identified two RCTs of gabapentin in ALS involving a total of 355 participants and performed a meta-analysis of the combined data.
S326: The trials were to short to provide data on survival a year afer enrollment, which was the primary outcome of the review.
S327: However, there was little or no diﬀerence in estimated survival at one year in the gabapentin-treated group versus the placebo group in either trial, or in the combined data.
S328: The arm megascore was the primary outcome measure of both trials.
S329: Results from the phase II trial showed a trend toward a slowing of the decline of arm megascore, even though this diﬀerence did not reach a statistical significance (defined as P < 0.05) (Miller 1996).
S330: Results from the randomized phase III trial (Miller 2001), as well as analysis of the combined arm megascore data from both trials, showed no therapeutic benefit with gabapentin in people with ALS.
S331: FVC, the other major outcome measure in these studies, is still an important outcome measure in ALS trials because of the importance of pulmonary function to people with ALS.
S332: Here again, there was little or no benefit from treatment.
S333: One trial measured monthly decline in the ALSFRS and quality of life using the SF-12 and found that there was probably little or no diﬀerence between groups for ALSFRS and quality of life.
S334: Drowsiness, light-headedness, and limb swelling occurred more frequently with gabapentin than with placebo, based on combined analyses.
S335: Fatigue and falls occurred more frequently with gabapentin than with placebo in one trial, but when we combined the data for fatigue from both trials, there was no clear diﬀerence between the groups.
S336: Overall completeness and applicability of evidence In the absence of trials of baclofen and other GABA modulators, the identified trials of gabapentin fit the overall requirements for inclusion in the review.
S337: One of the trials did not measure quality of life or ALSFRS (Miller 1996).
S338: Quality of the evidence We consider the two studies that were included to yield high- quality evidence that gabapentin is not eﬀective in ALS.
S339: We did not consider these studies to be completely free of any risk of bias, but both had adequate blinding, sequence generation and outcome reporting.
S340: We therefore assessed the risk of bias in these trials as low.
S341: We identified no reasons for downgrading the quality of the evidence based on the GRADE criteria of indirectness, inconsistency, publication bias, or study limitations.
S342: We downgraded quality of life and decline in ALSFRS outcomes for imprecision due to the small sample size (128 participants) of the one trial that provided data.
S343: Potential biases in the review process Review authors who were not investigators in the trials assessed risk of bias in the selected studies.
S344: DM was the statistician in both included studies and performed data extraction and entry for this review.
S345: He provided outcome data for checking by other review authors and the Cochrane Neuromuscular Managing Editor.
S346: Agreements and disagreements with other studies or reviews A Cochrane review reported that gabapentin had no benefit in trial participants with type II and III spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), which is another, more slowly progressive, motor neuron disease (Wadman 2012).
S347: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice According to high-quality evidence, gabapentin is not eﬀective in treating amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), because it neither halts nor slows progression of ALS.
S348: Gabapentin does not improve estimated survival at one year nor slow the rate of decline of muscle strength or respiratory function.
S349: Gabapentin probably has little or no eﬀect on quality of life or monthly decline in ALS functional rating scale.
S350: No evidence from randomized trials was available for other gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA)-modulating treatments.
S351: Implications for research Further studies of gabapentin to slow progression of ALS are unlikely to be fruitful.
S352: The literature provides no indication of alternative promising GABA modulators.
